rows.title: GDS Instruments - 1 WSE (Australia) - 050126
rows.description: • They have no local Australian entity, so they cannot legally run payroll or employ staff directly — this creates compliance and payroll risk and was repeatedly flagged; using an Employer of Record (EOR) is the proposed remedy for legal/payroll compliance. • Moving workers from visa subclass 417 to 482 is complex and likely to take about 6–8 months, risking staffing and operational delays; stakeholders should plan timelines accordingly and consider interim EOR coverage during the transition. • Setup and immigration costs are significant (~USD 15,000 / ~GBP 11,000), creating a material financial burden; team discussed potentially sharing costs with employees or negotiating phased payments. • Timing and visas (repeated): they need alignment with an October relocation and the ability to sponsor 482 visas while leveraging any 417 visa period. • Cost and transparency: total service cost and clear, itemized fees are critical. • Compliance and role fit: solutions must meet Australian salary benchmarks and match job specifications for regulatory compliance. • Key stakeholders and roles: Atlas legal will perform a feasibility review of the JD/role and confirm 417 visa and sponsorship pathways; Liam will present scenarios to the decision‑makers after the proposal. • Approval flow and evaluation criteria: share JD with Atlas legal → legal feasibility and visa/sponsorship assessment → le…
rows.notes: Pipeline: ✅ Sales Pipeline; Deal stage: Stage 1 - Discovery; Owner: Richard Sansone; Latest source: DIRECT_TRAFFIC

rows.title: Partner Referral-TXM Manpower Solutions
rows.description: • They receive recurring weekly requests for recruitment and contractor payrolling that Atlas cannot fulfill internally, specifically needing payroll/contractor-pay services in jurisdictions where Atlas only offers EOR; this creates an ongoing service gap that can delay payments, slow hires, and risk losing opportunities or creating compliance exposure. • No specific solutions were recorded; recommended next steps are to confirm which jurisdictions and volumes are most critical, and pursue short-term partnerships or referrals to local payroll vendors while evaluating whether to expand Atlas’s payroll/contractor-pay capabilities for high‑priority markets. • They will select a provider whose service mix (visa services, payroll, recruitment) aligns with and complements Atlas's EOR-focused model and whose pricing fits that model. • They will consult relevant departments and management after the requirement briefing, then schedule a follow-up meeting with broader stakeholders; approval appears to require management sign‑off, while specific evaluation criteria and a decision timeline were not provided.

rows.title: Freightsolution - 1 WSE (Sweden)
rows.description: • They are first‑time users of an Employer of Record and lack familiarity with EoR processes, creating operational uncertainty and raising the risk of setup errors, missed steps, and onboarding delays; they repeatedly requested onboarding support, clear process guidance, and hand‑holding from the EoR to avoid mistakes. • They must run payroll in euros and want to engage staff in the EU without visa sponsorship while avoiding tax and payroll exposure; this creates currency‑handling, payroll execution, classification, and withholding risks. Suggested remedies are securing a EUR bank account or using an EoR with EUR payroll capability, and obtaining local tax/payroll risk guidance to confirm proper withholding, classification, and compliance. • They require a provider who directly owns and operates all EOR functions, repeatedly emphasizing white‑glove service and strict compliance. • They need direct European account and implementation support aligned to their time zone. • They need to complete legal/contract approvals: review and approve the MSA and SOW, sign the contract, pay the deposit, and submit onboarding data plus the employment agreement. • Deployment is expected 10–14 days after contract signature (no visa required). • Final buy decision depends on the upcoming pricing and detailed-solution review. Best Service / Capability

rows.title: Partner Referral-Ruach Horizon
rows.description: • Partner onboarding, KYC and payment risk — They experienced partner misrepresentation due to weak initial KYC, which led to tax liability exposure when the partner went MIA. Bank delays and mismatched invoicing/payment schedules created cashflow and reconciliation friction, and giving broad employee platform access caused confusion and high support volume, so they reverted to admin-only partner control. Causes: inadequate onboarding/KYC, poor bank coordination, and unclear access roles. Agreed next steps: tighten KYC and contractual safeguards, pursue remediation for tax exposure, align invoicing/payment cadence (invoice by 10th, pay by 15th), centralize admin control and clarify user roles, and engage the bank to reduce delays. • Product access and performance blockers — The demo felt slow and encryption/permission errors prevented access to RFP documents, delaying evaluation and reducing confidence. Causes: demo/environment performance limits and misconfigured permissions. Next steps: run a repeat demo in an optimized environment with performance benchmarks/SLA, provide immediate technical support to fix permissions, and offer secure alternate delivery (staging access or secure links) for reviewers. • Commercial/pricing pressure — They are highly price-sensitive; competitor undercutting and internal margin constraints threaten the deal. Causes: competitive pricing and limi…

rows.description: • Immigration and permit risk: Frequent changes to Swedish immigration rules are creating planning uncertainty and stress, and they face a concrete risk that after EOR deductions their pay could fall below the Critical Skills Employment Permit threshold (~€42.44K), potentially jeopardizing eligibility; no mitigations or next steps were recorded. • Housing and relocation disruption: They couldn’t secure long-term rental in Stockholm and had to use Airbnb temporarily, increasing cost, logistical burden, and disrupting the move timeline. • Cost transparency and cash‑flow strain: Multiple separate fees (administrative, currency conversion, expat) make total costs hard to compare and opaque, while Native’s two‑month salary advance requirement for compliance creates significant upfront cash‑flow pressure; no alternatives were documented. • Strong price sensitivity: they expect low EOR admin fees (target ~$1.99–$2.99 vs ~$5.99 benchmark) and want clear FX/currency options (pay in local currency with FX fee only if conversion required, or USD with no conversion fee). • Favor low upfront commitment and flexible payment terms: prefer a one-month refundable deposit over a two-month advance-salary deposit, and need clarity on who ultimately bears each cost (employer vs. salary deduction). • Service scope and inclusions matter: they want explicit treatment of expat-specific fees, insurance…

rows.description: • Unclear employment model / EOR vs payroll: they don’t know who is responsible for EOR versus payroll, causing compliance and operational risk (potential tax/benefits errors, payroll delays, and confusion over cost ownership). Root causes appear to be ambiguous contracts and ownership gaps between HR/ops and external vendors. Recommended next steps discussed: clarify and document who owns EOR vs payroll, review contracts and local entity responsibilities, and align HR/legal with any third‑party EOR provider to update processes. • Invoicing and currency friction: they cannot invoice in CAD and are repeatedly concerned about constant‑currency exposure, which leads to billing delays, cash‑flow volatility, pricing disputes, and extra FX risk. Causes include billing/system limitations and contract/pricing terms that don’t support CAD or FX handling. Suggested remedies: enable multi‑currency/CAD invoicing or adjust contract currency clauses, establish an FX handling approach (hedging or pass‑through), and involve finance/ERP to implement the billing changes. • They will base the buy/no‑buy decision on Atlas’s pricing simulation and the submitted job description/supporting compliance documentation; a follow‑up review call will determine next steps, with a decision expected shortly after that review (within about a week). • Approval workflow: they submit the job description and compl…

rows.description: • They struggle to structure compliant employment contracts across multiple countries because they don’t have local entities, creating legal risk and repeated non‑compliance exposure; this is delaying hires and was raised repeatedly. Suggested next steps: evaluate Employer‑of‑Record or local entity options, redesign contracts to meet local law, and engage local counsel to close compliance gaps. • High inflation in Colombia makes pay stability a major problem — they need to protect employees from currency erosion but face legal restrictions on paying in foreign currency. rows.title: Blackstone - 1 Brazil 1 Mexico
rows.description: • Contracting and pricing uncertainty: they are seeing surprise billing, undisclosed backend fees, extensive redlines and an unclear gross‑up/tax mechanism that inflates overall costs and delays deals. Impact: budgeting uncertainty, stalled negotiations and fragmented context. Causes: misaligned commercial terms, decentralized change control and lack of upfront economics. Suggested fixes: require transparent, itemized pricing and no‑hidden‑fee clauses, agree a term sheet/heads‑of‑agreement early, centralize redline ownership and enforce firm negotiation timelines. • Liability and indemnification exposure: they are exposed to broad indemnification and an asymmetrical liability cap (Atlas benefits from a cap while they remain fully exposed), raising third‑party claim and loss‑allocation risk. Impact: greater legal and financial exposure and limited ability to limit damages. Remediation: narrow indemnities, seek reciprocal or capped liability, and negotiate clear tax/gross‑up language to limit open‑ended obligations. • Vendor onboarding and employment/termination risk: slow vendor qualification (e.g., an EOR failing internal risk/legal review) and inconsistent, country‑by‑country offboarding create missed timelines and difficulty exiting relationships; ambiguous employment clauses and fixed, disproportionate termination penalties heighten operational and legal risk. rows.title: Eteam /Santos (25+) Papua New Guinea
rows.description: • Hiring, compliance and cost blockers — they supply incomplete job descriptions and lack in‑house visa capability; operate under an entity/license that limits services to real estate and forces local-national hires; a key Santos contact has departed, adding implementation uncertainty. Impact: hiring bottlenecks, sponsorship/compliance risk, stalled expansion and higher staffing/visa costs. Causes: missing/unstable JD data, no internal visa support, regulatory/license constraints and contact turnover. Suggested next steps: collect complete JDs or provide a standard JD template, assign a single owner to validate JDs, engage legal/compliance to confirm allowable scope, evaluate an alternate entity or vetted secondment/subcontracting, and reforecast budgets for increased staffing/visa costs. • Process and stakeholder responsiveness issues — an unstructured referral process is overwhelming operational leads and risking churn; their Australian point of contact is unresponsive and partners (e.g., Hitachi) have prolonged sales cycles, leaving deliverables undefined and resources idle. Cause: unclear ownership, lack of SLAs and escalation paths. Remedies: escalate to senior contacts, designate accountable owners with deadlines, establish SLAs and regular checkpoints, appoint alternate POCs, and formalize the referral workflow. • Contract/legal bottleneck — a pending MSA legal sign‑off…
rows.notes: Pipeline: Co-Sell via Reseller; Deal stage: Stage 2 - Pre Sales Proposal; Owner: Melissa Harding; Latest source: OFFLINE
rows.roadmap_basis: Matched theme "Country compliance and profiles" via keywords: compliance, entity, visa, sponsorship. Roadmap items: Country Profile (Cascades compliance into the onboarding of clients); Integration of Country & Customer Profile (reduce indiv administration on customer level); Country Profile Default Settings; New Public Holiday Feature (API & Rollover).

rows.title: Expedient Staffing- 1 WSE Spain Benchmark Education New Business
rows.company_or_counterparty: Expedient Staffing- 1 WSE Spain Benchmark Education New Business
rows.description: • Legal/liability and local presence gaps: Gina reported they’re reluctant to proceed because imprecise indemnity language, undefined liability caps, and no local escalation/insurance expose them to overseas disputes and financial risk. Impact: hesitation to sign, potential legal/financial exposure. Root causes: ambiguous contract language and absence of local operations or coverage. Agreed actions: define liability scope and caps, rewrite/redline indemnity clauses, specify jurisdiction/dispute-handling or provide local support/insurance for intermediary activities. • Spain-specific employment‑cost uncertainty: unclear responsibility for post‑term payments (13th/14th month) and severance creates material budgeting risk. Impact: unpredictable termination costs and transition liability. Root causes: contracts don’t reflect local employment rules or allocate transition obligations. Agreed actions: clarify invoicing and payment obligations for months outside the core term, quantify severance/mandatory benefits, add contract language to cap or allocate transition liabilities, and consult local counsel. • Operational and compliance inefficiencies: manual time tracking and billing are error‑prone; existing VMO/contractor models increase misclassification risk and enable inflated vendor margins; lack of local HR entities complicates benefits and compliance across Spain, Argentina and…
rows.notes: Pipeline: Co-Sell via Reseller; Deal stage: Stage 7 - Funding Received (Closed Won); Owner: Melissa Harding; Latest source: OFFLINE
rows.outcome_signal: Stage 7 - Funding Received (Closed Won): Won reason not specified

Roadmap items: Invoicing Enhancements (Entity & Bank Details); Enhancement to Invoice & Payments; Collection Hub with JPMorgan & HSBC Integrations; Treasury Functionality (Forecasted Payroll, Cash Disbursement).

rows.title: Integralia Network-7 WSE 4Mexico 1 Columbia 1 Guatemala 1 Argentina
rows.company_or_counterparty: Integralia Network-7 WSE 4Mexico 1 Columbia 1 Guatemala 1 Argentina
rows.description: • Talent sourcing and execution risk: they halted an IT project after an AI-driven decision despite initial candidate traction; eClaro produced zero qualified candidates after three months; internal recruiting is slow, delaying ~20 critical hires and exacerbated by weak Q1 performance. Impact — lost candidate momentum, timeline and operational risk, and a depleted pipeline. Causes — limited internal recruiting capacity/processes, ineffective sourcing channel(s), and premature AI-driven stop decisions. Suggested next steps — escalate hiring priority, augment with external recruiters or temporary staff, reopen/replace ineffective sourcing channels, and review AI decision criteria and hiring SLAs to prevent premature project stoppages. • Cost, pricing and statutory exposure: they resist paying local burden while expecting Philippines-rate pricing; Mexico burdened costs (~43%) are compressing margins, and unplanned statutory costs (medical exams, severance, visa fees) risk budget overruns. Impact — margin erosion, budget surprises, and negotiation friction. Causes — mismatch between pricing expectations and true local statutory/one‑off costs. Suggested next steps — re-run cost models and benchmark local rates, review compensation/benefits or sourcing alternatives, compile and validate a list of statutory charges, build contingency buffers, and confirm requirements with local payro…
rows.notes: Pipeline: Co-Sell via Reseller; Deal stage: Stage 8 - Closed Lost; Owner: Melissa Harding; Latest source: OFFLINE; Closed won reason: Best Service / Capability
rows.outcome_signal: Stage 8 - Closed Lost: Best Service / Capability

rows.title: eTeam - Hitachi Energy- est 200 WSE's
rows.description: • Hiring, compliance and entity constraints are creating the biggest operational bottlenecks: incomplete job descriptions from Santos are preventing compliance checks, sponsorship progression and accurate quoting, while no in‑house visa capability forces use of external vendors and incremental cost; entity license limits and local‑national hiring rules further restrict service scope and raise delivery complexity. Impact: stalled approvals, regulatory risk, delayed hires, higher costs and reduced offerings. Causes: missing/ambiguous role details, no standardized JD template or clear ownership, and legal/licensing limits. Proposed fixes: require complete JDs with a standard template and examples, assign an internal owner to chase and escalate, build or source visa capability strategically (or use local partners/subcontractors), review entity/legal options (amend or form new entity), and adjust pricing/staffing or explore permissible remote/exemption workarounds. • Sales/partner responsiveness and process gaps are prolonging cycles and overloading operations: very long sales cycles (e.g., Hitachi) and an unstructured referral process leave resources idle, create unpredictability and risk attrition; an unresponsive Australian point of contact and slow partner replies further delay assignments. Impact: resource idling, missed timelines and strained operations. Causes: unclear owner…
rows.notes: Pipeline: Co-Sell via Reseller; Deal stage: Stage 2 - Pre Sales Proposal; Owner: Melissa Harding; Latest source: OFFLINE
